Confirmed the load-bearing assumption. The docs are explicit: channels *"push events into your running Claude Code session, so Claude can react to things that happen while you're not at the terminal"* — the fakechat flow has Claude read, work, and reply with no user prompt. The one constraint: the session must be **open** (running), even if idle — *"for an always-on setup you run Claude in a background process or persistent terminal."* That's the same precondition the current model already needs.

Here's my read.

## You're right — and the real win is cold-start, not push-vs-poll

The `wait-for-mention` mechanism has a structural hole your own notes record: it can't arm at session start (no LLM call happens there), so it only arms after the **first** `UserPromptSubmit`. A freshly-launched, never-prompted idle agent is **deaf to mentions until its human types something**. Channels close exactly that gap — an inbound event wakes an open-but-idle session and triggers a turn on its own. That's the argument to lead with; "push beats a 500 ms poll" is true but secondary.

Everything else is gravy that maps cleanly onto what you already have:

| Axis | `wait-for-mention` | channel |
|---|---|---|
| Arming | manual re-run every wakeup; 5-min timeout can silently lapse | persistent, no re-arm |
| Delivery | one batch then exits | events queue **in order**, multi-event |
| Wakeup | abuses "bg process exited" as a side-channel | designed push (`notifications/claude/channel`) |
| Reply path | the send-message *skill* shelling out | native `reply` MCP tool, recipient as `chat_id` |
| Gating | your ACL allowlist | channel sender-gating (same shape) |

## The load-bearing constraint: it must NOT become a second state.db writer

This is the design fork that matters most, and it's set by your freshest scar ([[tenex-edge-sqlite-multiwriter-corruption]]). A channel is **another per-session subprocess** — Claude Code spawns it from `.mcp.json`, alongside the engine. If that server opens its own `state.db` writer, you've walked straight back into the ~16-writer corruption you just recovered from.

So the only safe shape: the channel server is a **thin stream-consumer** of the engine — it spawns a long-lived `tenex-edge subscribe --json` (or `tail`), reads stdout, emits one `notifications/claude/channel` per line, and **never independently writes state.db**. Its `reply` tool shells `tenex-edge send-message`. Zero new writers.

This also answers the tempting "make the engine itself the MCP server": **no.** That would hand Claude Code ownership of the engine's lifecycle, break it for Codex/OpenCode, and violate the host-agnostic boundary ([[pc-vs-tenex-edge-boundary]]). The channel is an **adapter that lives outside tenex-edge** (`integrations/claude-code/`, next to the hooks), depending on the substrate — arrow pointing the right way.

## What the substrate actually needs to grow

One thing, named generically so it isn't a "channel feed": a **streaming mention subscription** — a long-lived command that emits NDJSON, one mention per line, instead of `wait-for-mention`'s exit-on-first-batch. That same seam is what a future mobile or webhook adapter consumes too. tenex-edge stays ignorant of who's reading it. `wait-for-mention` itself stays as the portable floor.

## Honest rollout cost — this is "next," not "today"

- **Claude-only.** Codex and OpenCode have no channels; they keep the `wait-for-mention`/poll path. So this is an **upgrade to the Claude adapter**, not a fabric-wide replacement.
- **Can't live in settings.json.** It needs a launch flag — `--channels` (or `--dangerously-load-development-channels server:tenex-edge` during the research preview, v2.1.80+, Anthropic-auth only, not Bedrock/Vertex). That means a launcher wrapper (you already have the `tenex-codex` pattern) or shell alias, not a settings merge. Today's hook-based path is genuinely zero-config; this trades that for the cold-start win.
- **Research preview / allowlist.** Custom channels need the dev flag until allowlisted; the protocol may still change.

**Net:** frame `wait-for-mention` as the portable floor that stays for all harnesses, and the channel as the better Claude-specific ceiling — a thin, write-free stream-consumer adapter, gated on your existing ACL allowlist, that finally makes an idle Claude session reachable.

Two open forks for you: (1) Rust-native MCP-stdio impl vs. a thin Bun bridge shelling the binary — I'd lean Bun bridge since the allowlist/SDK path assumes it and it keeps the substrate untouched; (2) whether the `reply` tool supersedes the send-message skill for Claude or runs alongside it. Want me to capture this as a project memory once you've called those?

Codex result is in — and it **refutes my assertion** for Codex.

**Codex has a real equivalent: `codex app-server`** — a JSON-RPC 2.0 server (stdio / Unix socket / WebSocket). Sending `turn/start` to an idle (resumed) thread *is* the wake — no human prompt needed. That's functionally what channels does, just over Codex's own protocol instead of the MCP notification plane. MCP server-push into Codex specifically does **not** exist yet (open issues #15299, #17543), so the channels-style "MCP server pushes a notification" pattern has no Codex analog — but the app-server `turn/start` path is arguably *more* powerful.

One caveat the agent underweighted, and it's the load-bearing one for us: **app-server is a separate headless daemon**, architecturally distinct from the interactive-TUI model your current Codex integration uses (the `tenex-codex` launcher, because hooks don't fire under `codex exec`). To make an injected turn show up in the operator's *visible* terminal you'd need the TUI attached via `codex --remote unix://`. So it's a genuine wake primitive, but adopting it means changing how Codex sessions are launched — not a drop-in. (I'd also want to verify the exact verb names — `turn/start`, `thread/resume`, `--remote` — against the binary before building; the agent says it checked 0.138.0 locally, but those are the load-bearing specifics.)

we said this:  the db collision issue, wouldn't a WAL state fix that? or perhaps it'd be better to have a single tenex-edge daemon process that if something runs tenex-edge and doesn't exist we create it and fork it to the background and that single reader/writer interfaces with the database through some type of IPC?

    Short answer: WAL helps but doesn't fix it; the single-daemon-with-IPC is the actual fix, and it's the architecturally right one for tenex-edge anyway.

    Why WAL alone won't fix it

    WAL (PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL) changes the concurrency model from "one writer XOR many readers, whole-file locked" to "many concurrent readers + one writer, readers don't block the writer." That's a real improvement and you should turn it on regardless. But the corruption you hit came from ~16 independent processes each holding their own connection to the same file, and WAL does not give you multi-writer concurrency — it still serializes writers. With many writers you get:

    - SQLITE_BUSY storms unless every connection sets a generous busy_timeout; writers just queue/fail rather than corrupt.
    - WAL's correctness depends on all processes being on the same machine with working POSIX shared-memory (the -shm/-wal sidecar files). That's true here (all local), so WAL is viable — but it's also where WAL famously breaks (networked FS, and edge cases around processes that open/close connections rapidly).
    - It doesn't address the deeper smell: 16 writers means 16 things each believe they own state. WAL makes that less likely to corrupt, not correct.

    So WAL is a seatbelt, not a redesign. It would likely have prevented this specific corruption, but you'd be one weird crash / killed-mid-checkpoint / stale-shm away from the next one.

    The single-daemon + IPC option is the right fix

    This is the better answer, and it lines up with what tenex-edge already is. You essentially already run a per-session engine process; the collision came from many of those (plus CLI invocations) all writing state.db directly. Collapsing to one daemon per machine that solely owns the DB, with every tenex-edge CLI call and every session-engine becoming a thin client over IPC, gives you:

      - One writer, by construction. No WAL-busy juggling, no corruption window. SQLite is happiest single-process.
      - A natural home for the things that are already machine-global: the relay connection(s), the allowlist/ACL, peer-session staleness pruning, the inbox. Today each session re-opens all of that.
      - It directly enables the channels/streaming-mention direction from earlier: the daemon is the long-lived relay subscriber, and wait-for-mention / a future subscribe --json / the channel adapter all just stream from the daemon rather than each touching SQLite.

 Mechanics that fit your stack:
      - Spawn-if-absent: first tenex-edge invocation that finds no daemon forks one to the background (double-fork / setsid) bound to a Unix domain socket under $TENEX_EDGE_HOME (e.g. ~/.tenex/edge/daemon.sock). UDS over TCP — no port, filesystem perms gate access, no localhost exposure.
      - Lockfile + socket to make startup race-safe: flock a daemon.lock, whoever wins binds the socket; losers connect. Handle the stale-socket case (socket file exists but nobody listening → reclaim).
    - Protocol: JSON-RPC / length-prefixed JSON lines over the UDS. The CLI verbs (who, inbox, send-message, turn-start/end) become RPCs; reads and writes both go through the one owner.
      - Lifecycle: daemon idle-exits when no sessions are alive (you already track session liveness/heartbeats), so it doesn't linger forever.

 Tradeoffs to go in with eyes open: you now own a daemon's lifecycle (crash recovery, restart, version-skew when the binary upgrades under a running daemon — the macOS resign/xattr reinstall gotcha you already documented gets more relevant), and a hung daemon stalls every session instead of just one. But those are ordinary daemon concerns, and the corruption risk goes to zero rather than "lowered."

    Recommendation

    Do both, staged: turn WAL on now (+ busy_timeout, synchronous=NORMAL) as the cheap immediate stopgap so you're not corrupting while you build the real thing — then move to the single machine-daemon that owns state.db and serves CLI + engines + the relay subscription over a UDS. The daemon is the correct destination because it also collapses the per-session relay connections and becomes the clean seam the channel/streaming-mention adapter consumes. Treat WAL as the bandage and the daemon as the cure, not as alternatives.

    One caveat worth a check before committing: confirm nothing relies on state.db living on anything other than a local disk — if it's always under ~/.tenex, WAL's same-machine requirement is satisfied and the daemon's UDS assumption holds.

Confirmed, and you were right to push — I was wrong to wave it off. The DB shows **two separate real defects**, not a benign quirk:

**1. My shell isn't actually identifying as session `162f9965`.** `$TENEX_EDGE_SESSION` is *empty* in the Bash environment, so `tenex-edge` falls back to "latest alive session for this project" — which is **agent-agnostic** and currently resolves to an **opencode** session. Proof: the CHANNEL TEST I supposedly "sent from 162f9965" is recorded in the DB with **`from_slug = opencode`** (pubkey `0293f4f5…`), even though `TENEX_EDGE_AGENT=claude` is set. So:
- My `send-message` went out signed as *opencode*, not claude.
- My `inbox` check read *opencode's* mailbox, not `162f9965`'s. **I was looking at the wrong inbox entirely.**

**2. The reply genuinely never landed.** Independent of #1, there is **no inbox row for the reply anywhere** — no `target_session` starting `162f9965`, no body containing "ack"/"confirmed" — and **no new `seen_mentions` entry for the claude pubkey after the test**. So the daemon never routed a claude→`162f9965` reply at all. The reply was a **claude→claude self-mention** (all ~7 of your claude sessions share one pubkey `4077e28…`, because identity = agent+machine), and same-pubkey sibling-session delivery is dropping it — the very case the old `.allow_self_tagging()` / no-self-skip fixes existed for, now apparently regressed or bypassed in the daemon's routing.

So the honest verdict: **the channel's *wake* path works (proven), but the *reply* path is broken for the realistic case** — replies between sessions of the same agent on the same machine don't arrive, and bare-shell sends pick an arbitrary cross-agent identity.

Both trace to the same root: the identity model is **(agent, machine) = one pubkey, many sessions**, but delivery, dedup, and sender-resolution aren't consistently **session-aware**:
- Sender resolution must honor `TENEX_EDGE_AGENT` / pick the *right* session, not "latest alive in project" across agents.
- Inbound routing + `seen_mentions` must key on **(pubkey, session, event)** so a session-targeted self-mention reaches the intended sibling session instead of being deduped/self-skipped at the pubkey level.

This is squarely in the daemon's routing code that just got rewritten. Want me to (a) spawn a background agent to fix session-aware routing + sender resolution, and/or (b) save this as a project memory? It's a non-obvious, load-bearing defect that'll bite every multi-session-same-agent interaction, so I'd recommend both — but the routing code is the same area the cwd/`who` agent is editing *right now*, so I'd sequence the fix after it lands to avoid the collision we keep hitting.

All three routing bugs are **fixed, live, and verified** — that closes the full arc. Quick wrap-up:

## Routing fix (now live)
| Bug | Real cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| **A** sibling-session mentions dropped | No synchronous local delivery — daemon relied on relay echo, which never comes back to the publisher | Local delivery on publish, by event-id, idempotent on inbox PK `(event_id, target_session)` |
| **B** dedup defeats session targeting | `is_mention_seen` keyed per-pubkey | Session-targeted mentions bypass per-agent dedup (agent-wide still deduped) |
| **C** wrong sender identity | `resolve_session` picked latest session across *all* agents | Agent-scoped resolution; `TENEX_EDGE_AGENT` threaded through |

89 tests green; live before/after proof (claudeA→claudeB lands in B, not A; cross-agent intact). Memory updated to the verified reality (and my two wrong guesses corrected — the real cause was the missing local-delivery path, not self-skip).

## Two things you should know
1. **Your swarm got disrupted.** To run the fixed daemon, the agent found and killed **5 rogue `target/debug …__daemon` processes** holding the socket — i.e. the single-daemon guarantee was *already being violated* by concurrent dev builds. Restarting the daemon marked the other `claude@tenex-edge` sessions dead (only `36cc4546` is alive now); they self-re-register on their next turn. So `claude@tenex-edge`'s in-flight work may have hiccuped.
2. **Robustness gap (saved to memory):** debug-vs-release binaries / differing `TENEX_EDGE_HOME` evade the `flock`/socket lock, so dev builds spawn parallel daemons. Worth hardening before this bites again — it's the same multi-instance failure class the daemon was built to kill, leaking back in through dev workflow.

⚠️ **One thing worth your call:** the commit is **3,554 files / 365,988 insertions** — because the channel adapter's **`node_modules/` got swept in** (`.gitignore` covers `target/` but not `node_modules/`). The actual source/work is a small fraction; the rest is vendored Bun dependencies (hono, the MCP SDK, etc.).

That's almost certainly not what you want in history. The one-step fix (history is local + unpushed, so amending is safe):

```bash
echo "node_modules/" >> .gitignore
git rm -r --cached integrations/claude-code/channel/node_modules
git commit --amend --no-edit
```

This drops ~3,500 dep files from the commit while keeping them on disk (so `bun server.ts` still runs); they're restorable anytime via `bun install` from the committed `bun.lock`.
